text: The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia and reflects the pelvic floor musculature away from the lateral aspect of the prostate using robotic instruments.
The surgeon facilitates the mobility of the prostate towards the apex, dividing the prostatic component of the puboprostatic ligaments.

The surgeon repeats the incision of the endopelvic fascia and reflection of the pelvic floor musculature on the left lateral aspect of the prostate, mirroring the previous actions performed on the right side.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to mobilize the pelvic floor musculature, reflecting it off the lateral aspect of the prostate.
The surgeon secures the dorsal venous complex with a suture, ensuring hemostasis in the area surrounding the prostate during the robotic-assisted prostatectomy.
A retracting suture is applied to the divided end of the uracus to ensure good hemostatic control during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies Cofalid retraction to the surgical field, enhancing the visibility and accessibility for further dissection of the prostate and surrounding structures.
The surgeon divides the anterior bladder neck, revealing the supraprostatic urethra beneath.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the bladder neck, aiming to fully identify the supraprostatic urethra, which is the segment of the urethra located just beyond the prostate.
The surgeon divides the bladder neck, revealing the supraprostatic urethra.
The surgeon uses a castor as a retraction tool to divide the posterior bladder neck, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues the dissection posteriorly to identify anatomical structures beyond the bladder neck, focusing on the supraprostatic urethra.
The surgeon identifies the vasa deferentia in the midline and the seminal vesicles laterally, preparing for further dissection in the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies hemlock clips to control the bladder wings, securing these structures during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the right vas deferens and retracts it, positioning it away from the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection between the two midline vasa, revealing the seminal vesicle located posteriorly to the vasa.
Hemlock clips are applied to the right vas deferens, securing it in place during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies and manipulates the artery leading to the apex of the seminal vesicle, focusing on its anatomical relationship with surrounding structures.
The surgeon lifts the seminal vesicles in their entirety, creating access to the posterior plane of the prostate, revealing the anatomical structures and facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection on the patient's left side, applying clips to the left vas deferens and retracting it, similar to the steps previously completed on the right side.
The surgeon retracts both vasa deferentia towards the anterior abdominal wall, creating a posterior plane between the posterior aspect of the prostate and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon develops a surgical plane anterior to the anterior leaflet of Denonvilliers' fascia, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes the fascial layer with the rectum visible behind it, indicating the anatomical relationship between these structures during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon retracts and peels away Denonvier's fascia from the posterior surface of the prostate, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon controls the right vascular pedicle, securing it to prevent bleeding during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the prostatic capsule and carefully teases away the neurovascular bundle on the right side, using the developed plane anterior to Denonvilliers' fascia.
The surgeon performs an intrafacial dissection, carefully separating the fascial layers surrounding the prostate using robotic instruments.
The surgeon uses Noquatry near the neurovascular bundle, aiming to minimize tissue damage and promote early functional recovery.
The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to carefully peel away the neurovascular bundle from the surrounding tissue, ensuring precise separation.

The surgeon continues dissection down to the apex of the prostate, mirroring the previous steps on the patient's left side, including peeling away the neurovascular bundle and controlling the left vascular pedicle.
The surgeon applies hemlock clips to control the proximal vascular pedicle, securing the blood vessels during the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the posterolateral contour of the prostate and carefully dissects the neurovascular bundle free using an a-thermal technique, avoiding thermal damage to the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the apex of the prostate, using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques to carefully separate the neurovascular bundle from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon adopts a high release technique to dissect the lateral prostatic fascia, carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs apical dissection after releasing the neurovascular bundle on both sides, focusing on the apex of the prostate.
The surgeon divides the dorsal venous complex, which has been previously secured, using surgical instruments.
The surgeon controls and dissects the apex of the prostate, carefully freeing it from surrounding tissues in a precise and controlled manner.
The assistant retracts the neurovascular bundle laterally, providing clear exposure for the ongoing dissection near the apex of the prostate.
On the right side, after the complete division of the dorsal venous complex, the urethra becomes visible.
The surgeon transects the urethra, following the apical dissection and division of the dorsal venous complex.
The surgeon highlights the catheter as it is carefully withdrawn from the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the prostate and places it into a specimen bag for removal, following its dissection and control at the apex.
The surgeon completes hemostasis using a 3-0 monocryl suture, ensuring control of bleeding in the surgical field.
After completing the previous step, the surgeon applies a flow seal, a topical hemostatic agent, to the surgical site.
The surgeon observes the surgical site before proceeding to fashion the anastomosis between the bladder neck and the urethra, aligning and suturing these structures together.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to perform the anastomosis between the bladder neck and urethra, ensuring secure tissue approximation.
The surgeon parachutes the bladder down towards the urethra, performing the initial anastomosis on the posterior wall following a posterior sphincteropexy.
The surgeon continues the anastomosis around the left lateral aspect of the bladder and urethra, progressing towards the anterior aspect, using a barbed suture.
The surgeon uses a second suture to continue the anastomosis from the right side, joining it with the previously placed suture on the anterior left aspect of the bladder and urethra.

The surgeon places reinforcing sutures on the anterior aspect of the bladder, approximating them onto the dorsal venous complex to strengthen the completed anastomosis between the bladder neck and urethra.
